title: Erdogan Claims Diplomacy Credit, Fidan Heads to Moscow, and Defense Tech Crosses the Atlantic
subhead: Erdogan framed Turkey as the voice of restraint during the Iran war, Fidan traveled to Moscow to tackle S-400 sanctions ahead of the NATO summit Ankara will host, and a Turkish drone firm signed a landmark co-production deal with L3Harris at Eurosatory — while the opposition remained under judicial pressure and the economy showed no signs of loosening.

sections.heading: Erdogan Claims Diplomacy Credit After Iran Deal
sections.body:
President Recep Tayyip Erdogan used a post-Cabinet press conference on Monday to claim credit for Turkey's stance during the Iran war, declaring that Ankara chose diplomacy over escalation from day one. 'We were not among those pouring fuel on the fire of war; we were among those raising the voice of peace,' Erdogan said, according to Daily Sabah.

Erdogan emphasized Turkey's coordination with Gulf partners and its support for mediation efforts led by Pakistan, alongside Qatar and Saudi Arabia. He described the US-Iran framework deal as an important step toward ending hostilities and easing regional tensions.

The president also used the occasion to highlight Turkey's upcoming role as NATO summit host on July 7-8 in Ankara, calling the gathering a milestone for the alliance's future security architecture. Erdogan rejected predictions of NATO's decline and touted Turkey's command of NATO's second-largest land force and its expanding defense industry as evidence of Ankara's indispensability within the alliance.

Beyond the NATO summit, Erdogan listed a string of international events Turkey will host in 2026: the NATO Parliamentary Summit in Istanbul on June 28-29, the 13th Organization of Turkic States summit, the 77th International Astronautical Congress, and the UN Climate Change Conference in Antalya.
sections.whyItMatters: Erdogan is positioning Turkey as both regional mediator and indispensable NATO partner — a dual role that will be tested at the July summit where S-400 sanctions and F-35 acquisition talks loom large.

sections.heading: Fidan Heads to Moscow as S-400 Sanctions Return to the Agenda
sections.body:
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan traveled to Moscow on Monday, with the S-400 sanctions issue returning to the fore weeks before Turkey hosts NATO leaders. According to Al-Monitor, Ankara hopes to reach a deal with Washington on the sidelines of the July NATO summit that could ease or remove CAATSA sanctions tied to Turkey's purchase of the Russian air defense system.

The S-400 dispute has been the single largest irritant in US-Turkey defense relations since 2019, triggering Turkey's expulsion from the F-35 program and sanctions under the Countering America's Adversaries Through Sanctions Act. Fidan's Moscow visit signals that Ankara is simultaneously maintaining its Russian channel while seeking a NATO-summit breakthrough with Washington.

Separately, a key cooperating witness in the US criminal investigation into Turkish lender Halkbank's alleged role in helping Iran evade sanctions is due to be sentenced this week, Reuters reported — a case that continues to cast a shadow over US-Turkey financial relations.
sections.whyItMatters: Fidan's Moscow trip and the Halkbank sentencing are twin reminders that Turkey's Russia and Iran relationships remain live liabilities as Ankara seeks to reset with Washington ahead of the NATO summit.

sections.heading: L3Harris and Turkish Skydagger Sign FPV Interceptor Co-Production Deal
sections.body:
In a notable defense-industry milestone, L3Harris signed a memorandum of understanding with Turkish drone manufacturer Skydagger on Monday at the Eurosatory trade show in Paris to establish co-production of first-person-view drone interceptors in the United States. The interceptors will be integrated into L3Harris's Vampire counter-drone system.

According to Breaking Defense, the framework involves a transfer of expertise and technology enabling localized manufacturing of Turkish-designed FPV interceptors. L3Harris's Mike Spina said the company aims to complete integration on the Vampire system this year — the first time the platform will carry interceptor drones.

Skydagger's Hunter interceptor weighs just over one pound and can fly at nearly 200 mph. The company can currently produce 50,000 FPV interceptors per year. The Vampire system has logged over 350,000 operational hours since 2023 and was selected by the US Army last week in a $106 million order as part of Washington's layered counter-drone defense approach.

The deal represents a rare instance of Turkish defense technology flowing westward: a Turkish-designed product being manufactured in the US for integration into an American weapons platform.
sections.whyItMatters: This is a reversal of the typical defense procurement direction — Turkish drone technology entering US production lines. It signals growing Western recognition of Turkey's drone warfare expertise and could open doors for deeper defense-industrial integration.

sections.heading: CHP Crisis Deepens as Court Ruling Ousts Opposition Leader
sections.body:
Turkey's opposition crisis continues to deepen following an appeals court decision that annulled the CHP's 2023 leadership congress, effectively deposing popular chairman Ozgur Ozel. The court ruled the CHP's 38th congress void from the beginning, overturning an earlier court finding that had rejected allegations of electoral irregularities, according to The Conversation.

The ruling installed 77-year-old Kemal Kilicdaroglu — who led the party to five consecutive election losses, including the 2023 presidential race — as interim leader. The move fits what Freedom House describes as a broader pattern of democratic backsliding and systematic weakening of the opposition under Erdogan's AKP.

As Time's Ian Bremmer noted in a piece published Monday, 'The latest maneuvers of President Recep Tayyip Erdogan signal that he has no plans to quietly exit the political stage.' The CHP's 2024 local election victories — controlling Ankara, Istanbul, Izmir, and Bursa — had made it the most viable opposition force in years. The subsequent crackdown, including the arrest of Istanbul mayor Ekrem Imamoglu in March 2025, suggests the judiciary is being used to neutralize emerging challengers before they can build national momentum.
sections.whyItMatters: The court ruling effectively resets the opposition to a weaker leadership at a time when Erdogan is constitutionally term-limited after 2028. Whether this backfires by galvanizing protest support or succeeds in demobilizing the opposition will shape Turkey's political trajectory.

sections.heading: Housing in Real Decline as Central Bank Holds Rates at 37%
sections.body:
Turkey's housing market extended its real-term decline, with the Central Bank's Residential Property Price Index showing prices falling 6.1% year-over-year in real terms in May — the 27th monthly real decline in the past 28 months, according to Daily Sabah. Nominal prices rose 24.5% annually, the weakest increase since May 2020, but remained well below the 32.6% inflation rate recorded in May.

Last week, the Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey held its key interest rate at 37% for a third consecutive meeting, halting an easing cycle that began in late 2024 as the Iran war's inflationary impact made rate cuts untenable. The bank raised its end-2026 interim inflation target to 24% from 16% in its quarterly report.

Istanbul University professor Ali Hepsen said the market is driven by deferred demand rather than speculative interest: 'High interest rates remain the main factor limiting prices.' Real estate economist Ahmet Buyukduman noted that house prices and real interest rates move in opposite directions — 'like two ends of a seesaw' — and predicted price increases will remain below inflation for at least the next year.
sections.whyItMatters: With the policy rate frozen at 37% and inflation targets revised sharply upward, the CBRT is signaling that monetary easing is off the table until post-war price pressures subside. For ordinary Turks, housing affordability continues to erode.

sections.heading: Turkey Opposes Iraq Pipeline Extension, Seeks Better Terms
sections.body:
Turkey does not want an extension of the existing Kirkuk-Ceyhan oil pipeline agreement under current conditions, a senior Turkish official told Reuters on Monday, after Baghdad asked Ankara to extend the deal for at least one year to allow more time for negotiations.

The pipeline, which transports Iraqi crude from Kirkuk to Turkey's Mediterranean port of Ceyhan, has been a recurring point of leverage for Ankara in its energy relationship with Baghdad. Turkey's position signals that it wants higher oil flow volumes or improved financial terms before agreeing to any extension.

The dispute comes amid broader regional energy realignment following the US-Iran ceasefire deal and the expected reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Turkey's leverage over the Kirkuk-Ceyhan route gives Ankara a bargaining chip in its broader energy transit strategy, particularly as it pursues alternative pipeline and rail corridors with Gulf states.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is using its pipeline transit position to extract better terms from Iraq — a pattern consistent with Ankara's broader strategy of turning geographic bottlenecks into diplomatic leverage.

sections.heading: Hejaz Railway Revival Gains Momentum as Hormuz Bypass
sections.body:
Two agreements signed in early June by Turkish Transport Minister Abdul Kadir Uraloglu and Saudi counterpart Saleh bin Nasir Al Jasser have given fresh momentum to the Hejaz Railway corridor project — a planned rail link from Mecca to Istanbul via Amman and Damascus, with potential extensions to Oman and Lebanon.

According to The New Arab, two successful test runs have already been completed from Turkey through Iraq to Saudi Arabia, confirming the corridor's viability. Joint feasibility studies are expected to conclude by the end of this year. Turkey has also signed a trilateral transport memorandum with Syria and Jordan in April, and is restoring railway lines near the Syrian border that have been out of service for 15 years.

While the project is generating excitement as a potential Strait of Hormuz bypass, analysts caution it cannot replace maritime shipping. Sea transport is roughly 10 times cheaper and handles far greater volume, noted Ussal Sahbaz of M&P Istanbul Hub. Turkish foreign policy expert Zeynep Gizem Ozpinar called the project 'a survival strategy' for Gulf energy exports, even acknowledging its inadequacies as a full Hormuz alternative. Professor Dr. Hasan Unal said it would be 'a bit too much' to think the railway could replace Hormuz with a land alternative.

The project aligns with Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 logistics hub ambitions and Turkey's Devlet tradition of leveraging geography as strategic infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hejaz Railway is unlikely to replace Hormuz, but it creates a strategic land corridor that complements sea routes and gives Turkey-Saudi economic integration a physical backbone — especially valuable during periodic waterway disruptions.

bottomLine: Erdogan spent Monday claiming diplomatic credit for Turkey's Iran-war posture while his foreign minister was in Moscow managing the S-400 problem ahead of the NATO summit Ankara will host in three weeks. On the defense-industry front, a Turkish drone company's technology entering US production lines via L3Harris is a small but symbolically significant reversal of the typical procurement direction. The opposition remains under judicial pressure, the central bank is stuck at 37%, and housing is in its 27th month of real-term decline. Meanwhile, Turkey is pushing back on Iraq's pipeline extension request and advancing a Saudi rail corridor that could reshape overland trade routes — all part of Ankara's pattern of converting geography into leverage.
sources: Daily Sabah, Al-Monitor, Breaking Defense, Time, The Conversation, Reuters, The New Arab, bianet, Bloomberg
